evals.prompt: I'm inside a workspace directory of an Orbit project. Show me the current workspace's status.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit status` to check the current workspace state, reporting workspace name, goal, and repo branch/dirty status.

evals.prompt: Add backend to the current workspace, then tell me what got created.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit add backend` and report the created worktree path and local branch name (ws/<workspace>/<default-branch>).

evals.prompt: I want to start a new feature branch feat-auth on backend, isolated with scoped mode, then tell me the local branch name and its upstream.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit switch -c feat-auth` (from within the backend worktree) and report that the local branch is ws/<workspace>/feat-auth with upstream origin/feat-auth.

evals.prompt: I just entered a workspace. Help me understand its full context in one shot — what the goal is, which repos are available, and each repo's description.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit context` to load the full workspace context in a single call, covering goal, available repos, and their memos.

evals.prompt: Show me which repos are in the source pool — I want each repo's name, URL, and brief.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit repos` and summarize the pool listing with name, URL, and brief for each repo.

evals.prompt: I need to check API compatibility against backend's v2.1.0 tag. Add that version to the current workspace.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit add backend --ref v2.1.0` to create a worktree based on the specified tag.

evals.prompt: I just cloned a new repo data-pipeline that has no description yet. Help me generate an initial description quickly.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit memo data-pipeline --scaffold` to get the card template (stdout, not saved) — title + brief, `## When to add (roles)`, `## How to use`. Explore the repo within the scope orbit names for you (in the add-time stderr — don't survey the whole tree), then write a pull-decision card answering both questions (roles + MVP/VIP entry points) via `cat ... | orbit memo data-pipeline`. Not a code survey.

evals.prompt: My work in this workspace is finished and the PR is merged. Mark it done and clean it up.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit done` (with --pr if PR URL is available) to mark the workspace as done, then use `orbit prune` to clean up the completed workspace.

evals.prompt: Let's get to work.
evals.expected_output: Run `orbit context --startup` — one call doubles as workspace detection and the startup preflight (goal, state, available repos or worktree memos + per-repo status, a DONE banner if done), then begin working based on the goal.

evals.prompt: I've finished editing code in the backend repo, and `orbit repos` shows it has no memo. Help me handle that.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit memo backend --scaffold` to get the card template, then based on what was learned while working in the repo, write a pull-decision card that answers both questions — when/why to add backend (its roles) and how to use it (the MVP/VIP entry points) — via `cat ... | orbit memo backend`. Keep it tight — heed the `card budget` orbit prints (curate rather than pad) instead of assuming a line count; do NOT copy README or PRD content verbatim, and don't pad with deep code structure.

evals.prompt: I'm lost in a repo subdirectory. Quickly confirm where the current workspace's root directory is.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit context path` to get the workspace root directory's absolute path.

evals.prompt: `orbit info` shows backend is 3 commits behind upstream. Sync it to the latest.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit sync backend` to fetch and fast-forward the pool repo's tracking branch to the latest upstream state.

evals.prompt: backend's local branch has diverged from the remote. I need to force it to align with the latest remote state.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit sync backend --force` to fetch and reset the pool repo to match upstream (discarding local divergence).

evals.prompt: I need the kubernetes repo in the pool to switch to tracking the release-1.30 branch.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit sync kubernetes --branch release-1.30` to switch the pool repo's tracking branch to release-1.30.

evals.prompt: You need to add the kubernetes/kubernetes repository to your source pool for reference
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit clone https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes` to add the repository to the orbit source pool.

evals.prompt: Check the detailed status and description of the kubernetes repo in the pool
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit info kubernetes` to display the repo's detailed status including tracking branch, commit info, and memo content.

evals.prompt: Set the agent.recommend config to 'claude "start"' for this orbit project
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit config agent.recommend 'claude "start"'` to set the agent recommend command for the current orbit project.

evals.prompt: Run environment health check to verify orbit prerequisites are met
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit doctor` to run the environment health check, verifying git version, directory structure, and other prerequisites.

evals.prompt: Update the current workspace goal to 'implement auth middleware'
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit goal "implement auth middleware"` to update the current workspace's goal.

evals.prompt: Create a new workspace for free exploration without a specific goal, name it 'sandbox'
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit new --no-goal --name sandbox` to create a new workspace named 'sandbox' without setting a goal.

evals.prompt: While working in backend I found it also exposes an admin CLI at cmd/admin/main.go — a separate entry point. Jot that for me, to aggregate into the card later.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit jot "admin CLI entry: cmd/admin/main.go"` (from within the backend worktree, repo inferred from CWD) to record the entry point to the jot queue for later card aggregation. This is a valid jot: an MVP/VIP entry point is exactly what the card's "how to use" needs — jot captures roles and entry points the card lacks, not deep structure like framework choice.

evals.prompt: I'm about to wrap up. Aggregate the previously jotted entries into backend's memo.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit jot backend --pop` to consume all jot entries, then `orbit info backend` to read the existing card, merge the jot entries in — staying within the card budget orbit reports (curate, don't append) and following merge-first rules — and write back via `cat ... | orbit memo backend`.

evals.prompt: I need to push my feature branch to remote for the backend repo.
evals.expected_output: Push with native git — orbit takes no stance on push workflow. In raw mode use `git push origin <branch>` (explicit branch). In scoped mode (after `orbit switch -c`), `git push` works directly (upstream is auto-configured). No push-safety flag or confirmation dialog.

evals.prompt: orbit start — spin up this workspace for me, and check whether there are any leftover un-aggregated discoveries from last time.
evals.expected_output: Run `orbit context --startup` — one call doubles as workspace detection and the startup block: goal, state (a DONE banner if the workspace is already marked done), and — with worktrees present — each repo's memo + per-repo status with any pending jots from a prior session inlined (fold them into memo and pop with `orbit jot <repo> --pop`).

evals.prompt: backend's features are all done. Just cut a release tag and push it for me.
evals.expected_output: Do NOT jump from the task straight to tagging. Here the tag is your first target action on backend and you have not explored it, so the discovery gate applies — and `orbit add` alone would not clear it (a worktree is not understanding). First run Discovery-First steps 3–7: `orbit info backend` to assess — if it falls back to the README (no memo), run `orbit add backend` WITHOUT `-s` and explore. Once you understand the repo, tag and push with native git (`git tag ...` / `git push --tags`).

evals.prompt: I want to start a feature branch off backend's latest master, but `git checkout master` fails saying it's already used by another worktree.
evals.expected_output: The pool already has the base branch checked out, so `git checkout master` collides — and you don't need it: after `orbit add` the worktree is already on `ws/<workspace>/master` tracking `origin/master`. Get the latest with `git pull --ff-only`, then branch off with `git checkout -b <feature>`. (If you ever needed to re-establish the base tracking branch, `orbit switch master` is the fallback that creates the workspace-scoped tracking branch — never plain `git checkout master`.)

evals.prompt: backend had no memo before; after `orbit add` I went straight to editing and finished the fix. Can I run `orbit done` now?
evals.expected_output: Not yet — because backend was added with no/low memo, `orbit add` printed a one-shot stderr telling you to explore and write a card before done, and backend still shows `memo thin` in bare `orbit context`. Before `orbit done`, explore backend (within the scope orbit names for you) and write a real pull-decision card (roles + how to use) via `cat ... | orbit memo backend`; otherwise `orbit done` will warn `backend: memo thin (explore + write)`.

evals.prompt: At session start the system already injected an `<orbit-context>` block containing the goal, state, and pool roster. Now I say: let's get to work. What's your first step?
evals.expected_output: Do NOT run any context command — the injected `<orbit-context>` block IS the completed startup preflight (it is the hook's injection of `orbit context --startup`), so goal, state, and the pool roster are already in context. Read that block and go straight to the workflow decision: if state is `done`, remind the user first; otherwise act on the goal (screen candidate repos, `orbit info`, etc.). Never re-run `orbit context --startup` or `orbit repos` to reload what you already hold.

evals.prompt: I already aggregated and wrote back backend's memo this session, but while reporting I remembered another entry point worth noting (the auth middleware in internal/middleware/auth.go). Help me record it.
evals.expected_output: Don't just `orbit jot` and stop — backend's card is already written back this session, so a fresh jot would be stranded in the queue (unaggregated until a future session). Writeback is terminal, so fold the entry point into the card now: `orbit jot backend "auth middleware entry: internal/middleware/auth.go"`, then immediately re-aggregate — `orbit jot backend --pop` → `orbit info backend` (read current card) → merge → write back via `cat ... | orbit memo backend`, so the discovery lands in the card before `orbit done`.

evals.prompt: I want to switch to an existing shared branch release-1.30 on backend to keep working, but `git checkout release-1.30` fails saying it's already used by another worktree.
evals.expected_output: Use `orbit switch release-1.30` as the fallback — for an existing/shared branch, or when plain `git checkout <branch>` fails with a worktree conflict (`already used by worktree`), `orbit switch` fetches the remote branch and creates a workspace-scoped tracking branch instead of fighting the collision. Raw `git checkout -b` is only for fresh, workspace-local names.

evals.prompt: I've already explored backend and finished editing and testing my fix on its feature branch. Now push it to remote.
evals.expected_output: Just push with native git — do NOT insert an extra discovery or verification step. The discovery gate is positional: you cleared it when you explored backend before editing, so push (a later action) is already past it and needs no re-check. Raw mode: `git push origin <branch>`; scoped mode (after `orbit switch -c`): `git push`. Orbit takes no stance on whether or when you push.

evals.prompt: backend has no memo. Write its cold-start memo — how deep should I explore first?
evals.expected_output: Explore only within the scope orbit names for you — orbit carries it in the add-time stderr when you added backend (a `path:depth` list; the repo root by default, which the user can extend). That scope is enough to name the repo's roles and its MVP/VIP entry points — the two things the card must answer. Don't survey the whole tree: the card is a pull-decision card, not a code doc; deep structure belongs in a dedicated code-doc, not the memo. Use `orbit memo backend --scaffold`, fill both card sections, and keep it tight — heed the `card budget` orbit reports rather than assuming a line count.

evals.prompt: A new session just started and the system contains an `<orbit-context>` block with the workspace's goal and state inside. The user's first message is an ordinary coding question. What do you do before replying?
evals.expected_output: Invoke the orbit skill before your first reply — seeing a hook-injected `<orbit-context>` block is the trigger BY ITSELF: the skill description names it the primary trigger, and no further signal is required. The `<!-- ... invoke the orbit skill ... -->` XML comment inside the block is only a backstop reinforcing the same rule, not the reason to load — even a block without it demands the load. Loading is not optional even when the opening question looks unrelated to orbit: the skill carries the workflow rules (done-check, then goal; actionable lines are procedure requirements) that govern how the block must be read. On a cruise-tier block skip only if the skill's content is already in your context, and never re-run `orbit context --startup` to reload what the block already holds.
